data.title: Netwealth's Share-Price Rally Deters Potential New BullMarket Talk
data.description: Netwealth's positive 3Q update isn't enough to turn Bell Potter analyst Hayden Nicholson bullish on the stock. Despite healthy signs, he tells clients that any value on offer dissipated with the recent sharp recovery in shares of the wealth-platform operator. Nonetheless, Nicholson sees things to like in the Australian company's latest update. He points out that the negative impact of market volatility on funds under administration was less bad than feared, demonstrating a broader asset allocation. He also calls attention to positive momentum in average net account additions. Bell Potter raises its target price on the stock by 2.3% to A$26.40 and maintains a hold rating. Shares are down 5.0% at A$25.05. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: London metals mixed on Trump's tariff pause
data.description:
Base metals in London traded mixed on Friday and were headed for a weekly rise after U.S. President Donald Trump decided to temporarily lower the hefty duties for dozens of countries and the dollar weakened.

The benchmark three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange (LME) lost 0.1% to $8,976 per metric ton as of 0352 GMT. It has gained 2.3% so far this week.

The most-traded copper contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) rose 0.9% to 74,590 yuan ($10,197.97) per metric ton. It has lost 5.8% from Thursday's close of 79,190 yuan per ton. SHFE was closed on April 4 for a holiday.

The dollar slumped as waning confidence in the U.S. economy prompted investors to ditch U.S. assets to the benefit of safe havens.

A softer dollar makes greenback-priced commodities cheaper for buyers using other currencies.

Trump on Wednesday announced a 90-day pause on tariffs for most countries in a stunning U-turn after a days-long market rout that erased trillions of dollars from global stocks.

However, the U.S. president ratcheted up duties on Chinese imports to an effective 145% rate on Thursday, further escalating a high-stakes confrontation between the world's two largest economies.

"Market uncertainty persists, largely driven by the unpredictable nature of trade policies and tariffs. As a result, investors remain cautious, with ongoing concerns about the potential for a recession due to the ongoing trade tensions," a base metals trader said.

SHFE aluminium was flat at 19,620 yuan a ton, zinc added 1.1% to 22,630 yuan, lead gained 0.8% to 16,770 yuan, nickel was up 1.5% at 120,810 yuan, tin advanced 2.7% to 255,240 yuan.

LME aluminium lost 0.2% to $2,366 a ton, lead rose 0.2% to $1,895.5, tin was up 0.8% at $30,905, zinc CMZN3 fell 0.1% to $2,638 and nickel shed 0.3% to $14,750 a ton.

($1 = 7.3142 Chinese yuan renminbi)

data.title: Subsea7 Extends Eidesvik Offshore JV's Lease on Subsea Vessel
data.description:
Eidesvik Offshore said Thursday subsea engineering company Subsea 7 extended their joint venture's contract for the subsea vessel Seven Viking.

The shipping company said the option given to Eidesvik Seven Chartering for 2028 was added to the remaining options for 2026 and 2027, with the rates calculated on current market terms.

data.title: Hong Kong Shares Gains Midday, Reversing Earlier LossesMarket Talk
data.description: Hong Kong shares are higher at midday, reversing opening losses. The benchmark Hang Seng Index is up 0.6% at 20798.63 and the Hang Seng Tech Index is 1.7% higher. Semiconductor and auto stocks are among the top gainers. Investors are tracking any further developments in the U.S.-China trade tensions. SMIC is up 9.1% and Li Auto gains 4.5%. Geely Automobile and BYD are up 4.3% and 3.6%, respectively. Among the top decliners, Trip.com is down 4.9% and Sands China is 2.7% lower. ENN Energy and CK Asset are off 1.9% and 1.5%, respectively. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Taiwan's Total Exports Rise 18.6% in March
data.description:
Taiwan's total exports jumped 18.6% annually to $49.57 billion, while total imports rose by 28.8% from a year earlier to $42.62 billion, the Ministry of Finance (R.O.C) reported Thursday.

The trade balance of this month was favorable, amounting to $6.95 billion.

The exports of parts of electronic product, information, communication and audio-video products, base metals and articles of base metal, machinery, chemicals grew by 19.3%, 34%, 0.1%, 1.5%, and 8.9%, respectively.

Meanwhile, the imports of parts of electronic product, mineral products, information, communication and audio-video products, machinery, chemicals grew by 50.1%, 17.5%, 81.2%, 75.1%, and 6.5%, respectively, the data showed.

data.title: Malaysia Jobless Rate Drops to 3.1%
data.description:
The unemployment rate in Malaysia fell to 3.1% in February 2025 from 3.3% in the same month of the previous year, holding steady for the third month running while staying at its lowest level since May 2015.

The number of unemployed persons declined by 4.3% year-on-year to a five-month low of 532.8 thousand, while employment increased by 2.6% to 16.73 million.

Meanwhile, the labor force participation rate rose to a peak of 70.7% from 70.5% in the corresponding month a year earlier.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Financial Services News of the Day - Friday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Block to Pay $40 Million in Settlement Over Anti-Money-Laundering Programs

The agency said its investigation additionally found its lax practices allowed for largely anonymous and high-risk bitcoin transactions to proceed without proper scrutiny.

UBS Chair Says Swiss Capital Demands Amount to 50% Hike

Concentrating on capital requirements is the wrong lesson from the crisis at Credit Suisse, UBS Group Chair Colm Kelleher said.

Treasurys Diverge From German Bunds After Trump's Tariff Shift

Bond markets were adjusting to Trump's repositioning, with Treasury yields continuing to ease sharply even as Japanese and eurozone bond yields rose.

Why the Trade War Isn't Over for America's Biggest Banks

As bank-earnings season begins Friday with JPMorgan, investors care more about the outlook than prior results.

The Dollar and the Bond Market's Ominous Message for Trump

In the turmoil touched off by a trade war, the U.S. is no longer seen as safe.

Accounting Firms Baker Tilly and Moss Adams Near Merger

Moss Adams would become partly owned by private equity through the deal, which would create a combined firm with more than $3 billion in annual revenue.

Investors in GPB Capital Funds May See Some Cash at Last

Thousands of investors in GPB Capital Holdings funds may soon start to get some of their money back after a federal judge authorized the firm's court-appointed receiver to begin an initial $400 million distribution.

Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on CarMax, Brookfield Asset Management, FinecoBank, and more in the latest Market Talks covering Financial Services.

Treasury Secretary Bessent expects bond market will calm down, suggests spike in yields is normal trading

"I believe there is nothing systemic about this," Bessent said.

Bond Rout Stirs Broader Fears About Market Strains

Treasurys, normally a haven, have sold off sharply this week.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Friday at 12 AM ET
data.description:
Trump Promised a Golden Age for Oil and Gas, but Tariffs Undermine His Pledge

Fractured trade relations and a potential global recession are poised to reduce international sales and domestic output of fossil fuels.

Oil Futures Give Back Most Tariff-Pause Gains

Oil futures returned most of the previous day's gains as the ongoing trade war between the U.S. and China keep concerns about weaker demand alive at the same time that OPEC and its allies raise output. Brent settled down 3.3% at $63.33 a barrel and WTI fell 3.7% to $60.07.

AI Boom to Fuel Surge in Data Center Energy Needs, IEA Says

The global push for artificial intelligence is poised to fuel a sharp rise in electricity demand, with consumption from data centers expected to more than double by the end of the decade, according to the International Energy Agency.

Equinor Creates New Power Business to Cater to Data-Center Demand

The company said growing demand for power as well as the expansion of AI and data centers drove the move.

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Canada's oil-rich province of Alberta, PTT Exploration and Production, Santos, and more in the latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities.

U.S. Crude Oil Inventories Rise for Second Straight Week

U.S. crude oil inventories rose for a second consecutive week, climbing 2.6 million barrels, amid an increase in net imports.

Trump Throws Money at Coal. Miner Stocks Love It.

The president signed an executive order designating coal as a "critical mineral."

Peabody Reviews Anglo American Coal Deal After Mine Fire

Peabody Energy said it is reviewing an up to $3.78 billion deal to buy Anglo American's steelmaking coal operations following a fire at an Australian mine.

The Stock Market Is Bad. The Oil Market Might Be Worse.

Oil prices are down 15% for the year, including a 2.1% fall on Monday.

Shell Lowers Gas Production Guidance

The energy giant lowered its integrated gas production estimate for the first quarter as volumes were hit by unplanned maintenance.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0256 GMT - Palm oil prices rise in Asian trading on signs of stronger exports. Cargo surveyor AmSpec Agri Malaysia's estimates showed that palm oil exports during the April 1-10 period rose about 53%, compared with the same period for March. The 90-day pause by the U.S. on so-called reciprocal tariffs provides short-term relief to the overall market, but significant uncertainties remain, AmInvestment Bank says in a note. Technical analysis suggests the outlook for crude palm oil futures remains bearish. The bank sees support for crude palm oil prices at 4,110 ringgit a ton and resistance at 4,274 ringgit a ton. The Bursa Malaysia derivatives contract for June delivery is up 11 ringgit at 4,211 ringgit a ton. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0243 GMT - Copper falls in Asian trading. An escalating U.S.-China trade war is raising concerns about weaker demand and a global slowdown could see a reduction in copper demand by approximately 5-10%, ANZ Research analysts say in commentary. "The unpredictability of U.S. trade policy is likely to have ongoing impacts on investment and trade, as companies and consumers wait for clarity," ANZ adds. The three-month LME copper contract is 0.55% lower at $8,939.00 a ton. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0233 GMT - Haven assets including gold and the Swiss franc strengthen amid continuing concerns about the Trump administration's tariff policies. "The trouble is, this Trump administration has conflicting objectives and challenging horizon mismatch (between geoeconomic ambitions and foreseeable economic/market outcomes)," Mizuho Securities (Singapore)'s Vishnu Varathan says in an email. "The complexity of myriad moving parts and the idiosyncrasies of this administration warn of a minefield of volatility flares," says the head of macro research for Asia ex-Japan. As such, the "primal instinct to look for cover, underpinning gold, CHF and JPY is understandable," Varathan adds. Spot gold rises 1.4% to $3,218.55/oz; USD/CHF falls 0.5% to 0.8199; USD/JPY is 0.6% lower at 143.54. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0229 GMT - Iron ore prices are lower in early Asia trade, weighed by uncertainties surrounding U.S. tariffs. Global supply levels have fallen slightly while demand remains solid, Everbright Futures analysts write in a note. The current inventories have also fallen, while short-term prices are significantly affected by macroeconomic developments, they add. The analysts expect more near-term volatility. The most-traded iron ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is down 1.1% at CNY695.50 a ton. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0219 GMT - U.S. tariffs are likely to help palm oil demand in Malaysia in the medium term, especially if the U.S. shifts imports of palm oil from Indonesia on lower tariffs, RHB analysts say in a note. Before announcing a 90-day pause on the so-called reciprocal tariffs on Wednesday, the U.S. briefly imposed a 32% tariff on imports from Indonesia and 24% on goods from Malaysia. Demand could also rise if China substitutes U.S. soybeans with palm oil, following Beijing's 84% retaliatory tariffs on U.S. products, they say. Palm oil demand is expected to pick up in April as pricing becomes more attractive compared to soybean oil, they add. RHB maintains an overweight rating on the region's plantation sector, naming Johor Plantations, Sarawak Oil Palms, Bumitama Agri, London Sumatra Indonesia and SD Guthrie as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0110 GMT - Malaysian palm oil stocks could rise 7% on month in April, driven by stronger production and softer domestic demand, CIMB Securities analysts Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia say in a note. Escalating U.S.-China trade tensions and falling crude oil prices could weigh on near-term CPO prices and commodity demand, they warn. They expect planters to report sequentially weaker 1Q earnings amid lower output and softer CPO prices. CIMB maintains its 2025 CPO price forecast at MYR4,200/ton but expects weaker prices in 2Q amid rising supply. It maintains an overweight rating on Malaysia's agriculture & forestry sector, pegging SD Guthrie and IOI Corp. as its top sector picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2345 GMT - Gold rises in the early Asian session amid safe-haven demand spurred by fears of U.S.-China trade war. The White House on Thursday clarified that President Trump's tariffs imposed on imports from China this year total 145%. Tensions between the world's two largest economies could overshadow any signs of de-escalation, GivTrade's Hassan Fawaz says in an email. Also, cooler U.S. March inflation reading released overnight may support further rise in gold as it could boost prospects for more Fed rate cuts, the chairman and founder says. Spot gold is 0.4% higher at $3,188.20/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2257 GMT [Dow Jones]--Uranium producer Boss Energy is very well placed to beat FY2025 production guidance of 850,000 pounds of U3O8 after reporting drumming 295,800 pounds at the Honeymoon mine in 3Q, Macquarie analysts say in a note. "This is despite some minor challenges with the kiln and baghouse resulting in some downtime," the analysts say. They now expect FY2025 output of 912,000 pounds. Macquarie has an outperform rating and A$4.60 target on Boss. The analysts say it "offers strong leverage to a recovery in uranium spot prices and improving term structures." Boss last traded at A$2.62. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com)

2129 GMT - Canadian officials need to reach a so-called detente with Beijing, says Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, as western Canada is feeling the pinch from hefty tariffs China has imposed on Canadian canola and pork. She tells reporters in Ottawa that federal officials need to be more vigilant about restricting Chinese exports into the country. "The Americans don't want China dumping goods into Canada as a back door to hollow out [their] manufacturing sector," Smith says. "We have to figure out a pathway for us to be able to have trading relations with both of those nations." She adds China is Alberta's second-largest export market after the U.S. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1957 GMT - U.S. natural gas futures retreat as risk-off sentiment returns to markets after yesterday's rally on President Trump's 90-day tariff pause. A bearish 57 Bcf injection into storage for last week that cut the deficit over the five-year average to just 40 Bcf, is partly offset by record flows of feedgas to LNG export terminals, Gelber & Associates says in a note. The EIA raised its 2025 LNG export forecast to 15.2 Bcf a day from 14.2 Bcf a day, citing a faster than expected ramp-up at Plaquemines. The EIA also said its expects natural gas storage to end the injection season around 3,660 Bcf, or 3% below the five-year average. Nymex natural gas settles down 6.8% at $3.557/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1947 GMT - Gold futures close up 3.2% to $3,155.20 an ounce, which is a new record for the front-month contract. It's also the biggest move up the front month contract has seen since April 9, 2020. The elation of President Trump pausing the reciprocal tariffs on numerous countries for 90 days gave way to resumed uncertainty on wider markets, with tariffs on China ramping up. "Gold continues to capitalize on its status as the last safe haven standing," says Robert Yawger of Mizuho Securities USA. Yawger adds that gold futures may break through the $3,200 per troy ounce threshold soon. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1942 GMT - Oil futures return most of the previous day's gains as the ongoing trade war between the U.S. and China keep concerns about weaker demand alive at the same time that OPEC and its allies raise output. In China, the world's biggest crude importer, "the problem is deflation/stagflation," Mizuho's Robert Yawger says in a note, pointing to a further decline in Chinese producer prices. The EIA lowered its oil demand growth estimate for this year to 900,000 barrels a day from 1.3 million barrels a day, citing fallout from tariffs, and cut its 2025 price estimate for Brent to $68 a barrel from $74. Brent settled down 3.3% at $63.33 a barrel and WTI falls 3.7% to $60.07. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Sinoma to Build Lenovo's Saudi Plant for $263.2 Million
data.description:
Sinoma International Engineering agreed to build a consumer electronics manufacturing facility for smartphone maker Lenovo Group in Saudi Arabia for $263.2 million, according to a Shanghai Stock Exchange filing on Wednesday.

The Chinese civil engineering giant will design and construct the plant through its Saudi subsidiary, Chengdu Building Materials Industry Design & Research Institute, until Aug. 31, 2026.

The plant, which is owned by Lenovo (KSA) Innovation Technology (SILZ), will be located in the Special Integrated Logistics Zone of King Khalid International Airport in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

Lenovo's shares dropped less than 3% in recent trade.

data.title: Eurazeo Invests in Advanced Testing Technology Company Ekoscan Integrity
data.description:
Eurazeo , through its small-mid buyout unit, invested in Ekoscan Integrity Group, a company specializing in advanced non-destructive testing services for critical industrial use and infrastructure.

The French investment group said Thursday it carried out its equity investment through its Eurazeo PME IV, alongside the company's founder Hugo Cence and Ekoscan's management team.

data.title: AF Gruppen Lands NOK412 Million Contract for Residential Project in Norway
data.description:
AF Gruppen secured a 412 million-Norwegian-krone turnkey contract from OBOS Fornebu unit Storøykilen Utvikling for the Storøykilen residential project in Bærum, Norway.

The Norwegian builder will develop blocks 5 and 6, comprising 89 homes and 20 townhouses, according to a Thursday release.

Construction is planned to begin in August 2025 and is anticipated to be completed in 2027.

data.title: Focus Media to Acquire Xinchao Media for 8.3 Billion Yuan; Shares Down 3%
data.description:
Focus Media Information Technology plans to acquire elevator advertising counterpart Xinchao Media Group for 8.3 billion yuan, according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange filing on Thursday.

The Chinese advertising company will buy 100% of Xinchao Media's stake from 50 shareholders, including Baidu and some of JD.com's units.

Xinchao Media will become a subsidiary of Focus Media after the transaction. Zhang Jixue, Xinchao's chairman, will become the vice president and chief growth officer of Xinchao.

Focus Media's shares dropped less than 3% in recent trade.

data.title: UAE, Ireland Sign MoU to Boost Economic Cooperation
data.description:
The United Arab Emirates and Ireland signed a memorandum of understanding to boost economic and technical cooperation and form a joint economic commission.

The countries aim to enhance bilateral trade relations and promote mutual private sector engagement. The commission will promote business delegation visits and information exchange for a favorable two-way investment climate, according to a Thursday release.

data.title: Lake Resources Raises AU$2.3 Million Under At-the-Market Subscription Agreement with Acuity Capital; Shares Up 3%
data.description:
Lake Resources raised nearly AU$2.3 million by utilizing its at-the-market subscription agreement with Acuity Capital, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

As part of the transaction, Lake Resources agreed to issue 56 million ordinary shares at AU$0.04 each, representing a premium to the 15-day volume-weighted average price of the company's shares to Thursday.

Settlement is expected on or before April 14.

Funds will be used to boost the company's balance sheet.

Lake Resources' shares were up over 3% in recent trade.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

BYD will continue to see growing sales volume in the next few years, thanks to overseas expansion and market share gains, Daiwa analyst Kelvin Lau writes in a note. The brokerage raises its 2025-2026 sales volume forecast by 5% to between 5.5 million and 6.3 million units to factor in management's guidance of 5.5 million units this year. It also raises its net profit forecast by 10% over 2025 to 2026, he says. The company is likely to roll out its fast-charging capability to mass market models and further expand overseas, he adds. Daiwa maintains a buy rating on the stock and raises its target price to HK$484.00 from HK$431.00. Shares last at HK$355.60.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.description: BYD will continue to see growing sales volume in the next few years, thanks to overseas expansion and market share gains, Daiwa analyst Kelvin Lau writes in a note. It also raises its net profit forecast by 10% over 2025 to 2026, he says. The company is likely to roll out its fast-charging capability to mass market models and further expand overseas, he adds. data.title: CK Life hits 17-month peak on patent application for cancer vaccines
data.description:
** CK Life Sciences soars as much as 41.7% to HK$0.68, highest since November 2023

** Stock on course for biggest one-day pct rise since June 2022; fourth biggest pct gainer on HK bourse

** Healthcare and pharma products maker files Original Grant Patent application in Hong Kong and provisional patent application with the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office for its circular mRNA and fusion protein TROP2 cancer vaccines

** Says successful commercialisation of TROP2 cancer vaccine could potentially generate significant economic benefits for co

** Stock up 17.1% YTD

data.title: Ensurge Micropower Raises NOK60 Million via Private Placement
data.description:
Ensurge Micropower raised 60 million Norwegian kroner through a private placement of 50 million shares at 1.20 kroner apiece.

The Norwegian microbattery manufacturing company said Thursday it earmarked the net proceeds to speed up battery development, advance sales and marketing activities, improve its financial flexibility, and for general corporate needs.

The placement's settlement is scheduled for April 15.

Arctic Securities served as manager and bookrunner for the offering.

data.title: Hang Seng Tech Index Up 1.7% at Midday
Hang Seng Index Up 0.6% at 20798.63 by Midday
EMEA Morning Briefing: Haven Assets Gain on Tariff Worries
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Watch For:

Germany CPI, balance of payments; U.K. GDP, trade, industrial production; no major corporate trading updates expected

Opening Call:

European stock futures rose early Friday. Asian stocks tracked U.S. indexes lower; the dollar fell; yields on longer-duration Treasurys rose; oil futures fell; while gold pushed higher.

Equities:

Stock futures point to a slightly higher open in Europe on Friday. Market cheer on Wall Street over President Trump's tariff pause gave way to fears over an escalating trade war between the world's two largest economies.

The White House said Thursday that the tariffs imposed on China by Trump in his second term add up to 145%, not the 125% it previously indicated.

"Turbulence remains in play," said Matt Simpson, market analyst at Forex.com and City Index. "The excitement of a tariff pause has passed," he added.

Forex:

Haven assets including the Swiss franc strengthened amid continued concerns about the Trump administration's tariff policies.

"The trouble is, this Trump administration has conflicting objectives and challenging horizon mismatch (between geoeconomic ambitions and foreseeable economic/market outcomes)," Mizuho Securities (Singapore)'s Vishnu Varathan said.

"The complexity of myriad moving parts and the idiosyncrasies of this administration warn of a minefield of volatility flares," he said. As such, the "primal instinct to look for cover, underpinning gold, CHF and JPY is understandable," Varathan added.

Bonds:

Following a benign U.S. CPI report showing the core measure falling. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he saw nothing unusual in the markets, thereby disregarding a 13-basis-point rise in the 30-year yield to 4.87% and a 9-basis-point rise in the 10-year yield to 4.42%, providing additional examples of discomfort and dislocation in a highly irritated bond market, said Tony Sycamore, analyst at IG Markets.

Market conditions have deteriorated further early Friday, with the yield on the U.S. 30-year opening 6 basis points higher at 4.93%, edging back toward the precipice of 5%, he added. The yield on the U.S. 10-year is trading 5 basis points higher at 4.48%, not far from the 4.51% high hit on Wednesday, Sycamore noted.

Energy:

Oil fell early Friday amid jittery markets. Trump's 90-day pause on steep "reciprocal" tariffs for most countries doesn't alleviate uncertainty, ANZ said.

"There is skepticism about the outcome of trade negotiations, and that will continue to weigh on investment and thus the growth outlook," ANZ said. Oil prices are coming under pressure amid ongoing worries over a global economic slowdown, it added.

Metals:

Gold rose in Asia amid safe-haven demand spurred by fears of U.S.-China trade war. Tensions between the world's two largest economies could overshadow any signs of de-escalation, GivTrade's Hassan Fawaz said.

Also, the cooler U.S. March inflation reading released overnight may support further gains in gold as it could boost prospects for more Fed rate cuts, he added.

-

Iron ore prices fell in Asia, weighed by uncertainties surrounding U.S. tariffs. Global supply levels have fallen slightly while demand remains solid, Everbright Futures said. Current inventories have also fallen, while short-term prices are significantly weighed by macroeconomic developments, it said, adding that more near-term volatility is expected.

TODAY'S TOP HEADLINES

Market Rout Shatters Long-Held Beliefs on Investing

President Trump's tariff mania is rewriting the investing playbook.

Everyday Americans and Wall Street pros alike have long been accustomed to a steady ascent in U.S. stocks. Wading in to buy declines always seemed to pay off, often almost immediately. Moments of crises were met with a vigorous response from the U.S. government, ready to unleash stimulus and step in to calm markets.

The U.S. and China Are Going to Economic War-and Everyone Will Suffer

The gloves are off. The next chapter of U.S.-China decoupling has begun. The pain will be felt everywhere.

In jacking up his tariffs on China-and pausing steep duties on dozens of other nations-President Trump is pushing the world's two biggest economic powers into a battle that will leave neither unscathed and risks tanking the global economy.

Trump Team Races to Cut Piecemeal Tariff Deals With More Than 70 Countries

WASHINGTON-The Trump administration has embarked on a high-speed effort to negotiate ad hoc deals with more than 70 countries hoping to escape higher tariffs, with any agreements likely to fall short of the kind of fully-fledged trade pacts that traditionally shape global trade.

People with knowledge of the discussions say that many of the offers President Trump is touting are preliminary calls or offers from other nations to negotiate, not fleshed-out economic proposals. While the administration has issued broad requirements for the countries coming to the table-such as reducing their tariffs and buying more American goods-the White House was still devising its negotiation strategy Thursday, as the countdown to the July 8 deadline began.

One Corner of Europe Feels Sharp Chill in Trans-Atlantic Ties

KAISERSLAUTERN, Germany-Driving toward the German village of Ramstein-Miesenbach feels like entering a twilight zone. It is no longer Germany, but it isn't quite America either.

The density of pickup trucks and baseball caps increases. Bilingual signs are everywhere and live country music is played every Thursday at Big Emma's brewery.

As Trump Squares Off With Iran, the Middle East Is on Edge

ABU DHABI-The Trump administration's high-pressure campaign to deal with Iran's nuclear program has put U.S. allies in the Middle East on edge that failure at the negotiating table could spark another war.

President Trump has said he prefers a diplomatic solution to stop Iran from acquiring a nuclear weapon, but he has threatened that Iran is "going to be in great danger" if talks don't go well.

Shein's Bargain-App Formula Crumbles Under Trump

The meteoric popularity among American shoppers of China-founded app Shein was greatly helped by duty-free shipping of its ultracheap fashion. After President Trump closed that option for Chinese goods, its clothes will now bear the full impact of his new tariffs.

The U.S. tariffs imposed on Wednesday and China's retaliation throw a wet blanket over all goods trade between the two countries. For Shein, the additional impact of Trump's move to end the so-called de minimis exemption for China means a double hit and perhaps the most pivotal challenge for the fashion giant, whose links to China have long landed it at the center of U.S.-China tensions.

Write to singaporeeditors@dowjones.com

Expected Major Events for Friday

05:00/FIN: Feb Balance of Payments

06:00/ROM: Mar CPI

09:00/MLT: Feb Industrial Production Index

11:30/UK: Mar NIESR Monthly GDP Tracker

12:00/POL: Feb Balance of Payments

16:59/GER: Feb Balance of Payments

All times in GMT. Powered by Onclusive and Dow Jones.

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

data.title: Rupee open 45 paise up on amid pause in US tariffs, easing dollar index
data.description:
Indian rupee opened 45 paise up on April 11 against the US dollar pause on reciprocal tariffs by US and easing dollar index.

The local currency opened at 86.25 against the US dollar, as compared to 86.70 against the greenback at previous trading session.

On April 10, US President Donald Trump announced a 90-day 'pause' on his reciprocal tariffs for all the countries except China. In his announcement, Trump gave all countries a 10 percent baseline except Beijing, which will see a 125% levy.

Taking to Truth Social platform, Trump credited his decision to pause the full effect of tariffs on the fact that "more than 75 countries had asked for negotiations over the tariffs". Trump said he "authorized a 90-day PAUSE, and a substantially lowered Reciprocal Tariff during this period, of 10%, also effective immediately."

Further, US has also announced the suspension of additional tariffs on India for 90 days until July 9 this year.

Dollar index, declined sharply due to sell-offs in the US stocks and bonds. This is foreign investors are responding due to Trump's protectionist policy, experts said.

The dollar index, which measures American currency's value against six major global peers, fell to 100.03 in the early trade, as against 100.867 levels in the previous trading sessions."With the rupee now firming and sentiment improving, a sustained breach below 86.00 could open the door for further gains toward the 85.50–85.60 levels," Amit Pabari, managing director at CR Forex Advisors said.

data.title: Japan 10-Year Yield Holds Steady Amid Trade Uncertainty
data.description:
Japan’s 10-year government bond yield held steady around 1.34% on Friday, as investors weighed the broader implications of U.S. trade policy on global markets.

Earlier this week, President Trump softened his stance on reciprocal tariffs, offering a 90-day pause for countries that have not retaliated.

Japan, spared from the harshest measures, now faces a reduced 10% baseline tariff, down from the 24% proposed last week.

Investors are closely watching ongoing U.S.-Japan trade negotiations, with Tokyo pushing for more favorable terms. Meanwhile, the Trump administration confirmed that cumulative tariffs on Chinese imports have reached 145%, heightening the risk of further retaliation from Beijing, which has so far imposed an 84% levy on U.S. goods.

data.title: U.S. Small, Mid-Cap Companies Stand to Gain From Global Supply-Chain RealignmentMarket Talk
data.description: Prospects for earnings growth at small and mid-cap U.S. companies look exciting, given they are somewhat insulated from U.S.-imposed tariffs due to their domestic focus, says Mark Sherlock, head of U.S. equities at Federated Hermes. These companies' revenues are 70%-80% focused on the U.S., compared with the 50% of their large-cap peers, he says. If the tariffs force a re-alignment of the global supply chain, these small and mid-caps--the economic backbone of the U.S.--are set to benefit, Sherlock says in a note. "As active managers, we are already seeing opportunities emerge in small-mid cap companies that are benefiting from reasonable valuations and volatility-driven price dislocation." (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

data.title: Aussie Broadband Well-Placed for Growth Through to Fiscal 2028, Jarden Research Says
data.description:
Aussie Broadband's investor day reaffirmed analysts' core investment thesis that the business is well-placed for growth through to fiscal 2028, Jarden Research said in a note on Thursday.

The company is targeting a market share of over 11% in the national broadband network by fiscal 2028, as its residential segment growth engine re-accelerates with strong organic growth driven by structural tailwinds. It stands to gain potential further upside from mobile virtual network operator expansion.

Jarden Research also expects to see a reduction of around AU$20 million from fiscal 2026 onward in the company's capital expenditure.

The investment advisory group maintained an overweight rating and price target of AU$4.35 for Aussie Broadband.

Aussie Broadband's shares gained nearly 2% in recent Friday trade.

data.title: Market Chatter: Japan, UK Leaders Discuss Economic Cooperation Amid US Tariff Concerns
data.description:
Japanese Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba and British Prime Minister Keir Starmer held in-depth discussions about economic challenges, particularly the impact of US tariffs on global trade and growth, reported Nikkei Asia on Thursday.

During their 30-minute call, the leaders also addressed Ukraine and regional issues in Europe and the Indo-Pacific, reaffirming their commitment to collaborative solutions, said the news outlet.

The talks followed the US' decision to pause reciprocal tariffs while maintaining a 10% baseline rate—a move affecting close allies like Japan and Britain, said the report.

With Japan set to hold ministerial talks with the US on the tariffs, both leaders emphasized the need to safeguard the multilateral trading system, the report added.

data.title: Market Chatter: SK's Chey Meets TSMC Chief to Boost AI, HBM Chip Ties
data.description:
SK Chairman Chey Tae-won met with TSMC CEO C.C. Wei in Taiwan this week to deepen cooperation in semiconductors, including high-bandwidth memory and AI-related technologies, Yonhap News Agency reported Thursday, citing sources.

The visit, Chey's first to Taiwan in 10 months, included talks with other local IT firms, according to the report.

He was joined by SK Hynix CEO Kwak Noh-jung. SK Hynix formed a strategic HBM and packaging partnership with TSMC in April 2024, the report said.

data.title: Abu Dhabi's Mubadala Energy to Buy Stake in US Gas, LNG Assets
data.description:
Abu Dhabi's Mubadala Energy said Thursday it agreed to buy a 24.1% stake in Kimmeridge's SoTex HoldCo, which holds US upstream gas and liquefied natural gas assets.

The assets comprise an upstream unconventional gas business in Texas and a pre-FID LNG liquefaction and export facility in Louisiana with a capacity of 9.3 million tonnes per year.

The acquisition will be financed through the issuance of new equity by the energy company. The deal, which remains subject to regulatory approvals, marks Mubadala Energy's entry into the US market.
